title: Dynamic Array Reversal
prompt_md:
## Goal

The number of values is not known until the program runs, so you must size the storage at runtime. Allocate a dynamic array, read the values into it, reverse their order **in place**, and print them.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100), the count of elements.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.

## Output

- One line: the elements in reversed order, each followed by a single space (including a trailing space after the last element).

## Constraints

- Use a runtime-sized dynamic array (`new[]` / `delete[]`); free it before exiting. Reverse the existing array in place rather than building a new one. No `std::vector`, `std::array`, or library reverse/sort.

title: Merge Two Dynamic Arrays
prompt_md:
## Goal

You are given two already-sorted sequences whose sizes are only known at runtime. Combine them into a single sorted sequence held in a third dynamic array, then print it. The combined array must end up in non-decreasing order without ever calling a sort routine.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `a` (1 <= a <= 50), the size of the first array.
- Line 2: `a` space-separated integers in non-decreasing order.
- Line 3: an integer `b` (1 <= b <= 50), the size of the second array.
- Line 4: `b` space-separated integers in non-decreasing order.

## Output

- One line: all `a + b` integers in non-decreasing order, each followed by a single space (including a trailing space after the last element).

## Constraints

- All three arrays are runtime-sized via `new[]`; free each with `delete[]`. Produce the merged order by comparing front elements of the two inputs, not by concatenating and sorting. No `std::vector`, `std::array`, or library merge/sort.

test_cases.label: Basic merge of two small arrays
All of one array precedes the other
